command.text: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print0 | while IFS= read -r -d '' f; do chmod 755 "$f"; echo "$f"; done | wc -l

raw_output_payload.content: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print0 | while IFS= read -r -d '' f; do chmod 755 "$f"; echo "$f"; done | wc -l
raw_output_payload.reasoning: Need find all .php under /testbed, chmod 755, count. Use find -type f -name '*.php' -exec chmod 755 {} +; then count with wc -l. But need to print number changed. We can use find -print0 | tee? Simpler: find ... -print | xargs chmod 755; then count lines. Use find ... -print | tee /dev/null | wc -l? Actually we need count of files changed. We can use find ... -print0 | xargs -0 chmod 755; then use find ... -print | wc -l separately? But that would run twice. Better: use find ... -print0 | tee /dev/null | wc -l? But tee will output file names. We can use find ... -print0 | xargs -0 chmod 755; then use find ... -type f -name '*.php' | wc -l? But that counts again. Maybe use find ... -print0 | while read -d '' f; do chmod 755 "$f"; done; echo $?? But need count. Use find ... -print0 | while read -d '' f; do chmod 755 "$f"; echo "$f"; done | wc -l. That prints count. So command: find /testbed -type f -name '*.php' -print0 | while IFS= read -r -d '' f; do chmod 755 "$f"; echo "$f"; done | wc -l. That outputs number. Let's output that.
